description: The provided image displays a publisher 'Check for updates' badge featuring a bookmark icon inside a multicolored ring, which possesses no scientific content, experimental data, microstructures, spectra, or quantitative curves relevant to materials research. Because the image pixels reveal only this non-scientific graphic and the caption is empty, no sample designations, processing conditions, phase distributions, or property values can be visually verified. The accompanying textual context indicates that the paper investigates tension-tension fatigue behavior of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel and includes figures addressing stress-strain response, S-N fatigue curves, SEM fracture surfaces, EBSD crack-path mapping, and TEM deformation microstructures related to κ-carbide precipitation states; however, the specific scientific identity and content of this Figure remain undetermined from the available visual information.

Figure 1 displays a 3D schematic rendering of the flat dog-bone-shaped specimen geometry and critical dimensions employed for tensile and fatigue tests on Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel. Visible labels indicate a total length of 55 mm, a gauge length of 15 mm, a gauge width of 5 mm, a specimen thickness of 2.5 mm, transition lengths of 15 mm, and symmetrical fillet radii of R13 mm, with a grip-end width of 7 mm. The caption states that specimens were machined by wire electrical discharge machining with the loading axis parallel to the rolling direction, and that identical dimensions were used for both sample types. Subsequent mechanical polishing and electro-polishing were applied to ensure stress-free surfaces. This standardized geometry underpins the experimental comparison of room-temperature tensile response (constant strain rate of 10^-3 s^-1) and stress-controlled tension-tension fatigue life (sinusoidal loading at 20 Hz, stress ratio R = 0.1), establishing the specimen-level basis for evaluating how intragranular and intergranular κ-carbide precipitation states influence the mechanical performance of the studied steel.

Figure 2 presents the microstructural evolution of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under different heat treatments. Panel (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation map of the solution-treated (ST) sample, revealing a fully austenitic FCC grain structure colored by crystallographic orientation with a 100 μm scale bar. Panels (b)-(d) are bright-field TEM micrographs of aged samples 550-6, 600-6, and 650-6, respectively, each including a [001] zone-axis SAED inset indexing γ-austenite (γ) and κ-carbide (κ) reflections. Panel (b) at 0.5 μm scale shows uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbides within austenite grains after aging at 550 °C for 6 h. Panel (c) at 0.5 μm scale displays the 600 °C aged condition, where orange arrows indicate intergranular κ-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries coexisting with intragranular particles. Panel (d) at 1 μm scale reveals pronounced coarse intergranular κ-carbides along boundaries in the 650 °C aged sample. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that increasing aging temperature transforms κ-carbide morphology from uniform intragranular distribution to progressively coarsened intergranular boundary precipitation, providing direct microstructural evidence linking precipitation state to fatigue behavior.

This figure presents a line plot quantifying the intragranular κ-carbide precipitate size as a function of aging temperature for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel subjected to 6 h of aging. The horizontal axis indicates aging temperature at 550, 600, and 650 °C, while the vertical axis gives the intragranular κ-carbide size from 0 to 30 nm. A single data series, labeled 'Aged for 6 h,' plots mean values connected by a straight line, with vertical error bars representing standard deviation as noted in the caption. The measured precipitate sizes increase monotonically and approximately linearly with rising temperature: from roughly 11 nm at 550 °C, to about 18 nm at 600 °C, and further to approximately 24 nm at 650 °C. This systematic coarsening behavior indicates that elevated aging temperatures accelerate the growth of intragranular κ-carbides while maintaining their morphology. The figure supports the core scientific conclusion that aging temperature is a critical processing parameter controlling κ-carbide dimensions, thereby establishing the microstructural basis for interpreting the non-monotonic fatigue property variations reported for this alloy system.

Figure 4 displays tensile engineering stress–strain curves for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under four heat-treatment conditions: solution-treated (ST, green), and aged at 550 °C/6 h (550-6, black), 600 °C/6 h (600-6, red), and 650 °C/6 h (650-6, blue). The ST curve shows the lowest strength and highest ductility. All aged samples demonstrate substantially increased yield and ultimate tensile strengths compared with ST, accompanied by reduced elongation. The 600-6 curve reaches the highest ultimate tensile strength, while 550-6 exhibits comparable high strength; by contrast, the 650-6 curve lies distinctly lower. Caption and text report that the strengthening arises from precipitation hardening via intragranular κ-carbides impeding dislocations, whereas the 650-6 strength drop results from grain-boundary weakening induced by severe intergranular κ-carbide precipitation, which disrupts boundary continuity and promotes early damage despite remaining intragranular precipitates.

Figure 5 presents the stress amplitude versus number of reversals to fracture (Δσ/2–2Nf) fatigue curves for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under four heat-treatment conditions: solution-treated (ST, green inverted triangles), and aged at 550 °C/6 h (550-6, black squares), 600 °C/6 h (600-6, red circles), and 650 °C/6 h (650-6, blue triangles). Plotted in double-logarithmic coordinates, all four curves exhibit approximately linear behavior in the finite-life regime before leveling off into distinct fatigue limits. The fitted curves demonstrate that all aged samples achieve longer fatigue lives than the ST sample at a given stress amplitude. Quantitatively, the fatigue strengths at 10^7 cycles are 157.5 MPa for ST, 162 MPa for 550-6, 175.5 MPa for 600-6, and 171 MPa for 650-6. Notably, the 550-6 condition delivers the longest fatigue lives at higher stress amplitudes, whereas the 600-6 condition attains the highest fatigue lives and fatigue strength at lower stress amplitudes near the fatigue limit. This figure supports the central conclusion that aging-induced κ-carbide precipitation enhances fatigue resistance, with the competition between intragranular precipitation strengthening and grain boundary weakening governing the divergence between high-stress and low-stress fatigue behavior.

This figure presents a series of eight SEM micrographs (a–h) illustrating the macroscopic fatigue fracture surfaces of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under stress amplitudes of 180 MPa (a–d) and 315 MPa (e–h). The samples include solution-treated (ST, a,e) and aged conditions at 550 °C/6 h (550-6, b,f), 600 °C/6 h (600-6, c,g), and 650 °C/6 h (650-6, d,h), with a scale bar of 500 μm. Yellow dashed curves mark the boundaries of the crack initiation and propagation zone, and the inscribed values denote their areal fraction f_A. At 180 MPa, f_A increases from 0.53 (ST) to 0.62 (550-6), 0.74 (600-6), and 0.63 (650-6), whereas at 315 MPa the values are 0.25 (ST), 0.55 (550-6), 0.43 (600-6), and 0.32 (650-6). The enlarged crack-initiation/propagation area in optimally aged samples—600-6 at low stress and 550-6 at high stress—correlates with enhanced fatigue resistance, while over-aging at 650 °C diminishes this beneficial effect. These observations demonstrate that the κ-carbide precipitation state critically governs fatigue damage tolerance by controlling crack nucleation and growth morphology.

Figure 7 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fatigue crack propagation zones in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel, comparing solution-treated (ST) and aged samples (550-6, 600-6, 650-6) tested at stress amplitudes of 180 MPa (a-d) and 315 MPa (e-h). Each subfigure includes a higher-magnification inset with a 10 μm scale bar revealing detailed fatigue striation morphology, while the main images use a 100 μm scale bar. All specimens exhibit well-defined fatigue striations characteristic of transgranular crack advancement. The 550-6 and 600-6 samples display more pronounced and closely spaced striations relative to ST and 650-6, with caption-reported narrowest spacing in 600-6 at 180 MPa and 550-6 at 315 MPa. The 650-6 condition shows comparatively wider striation spacing, consistent with coarse intergranular κ-carbide precipitation accelerating crack growth. These morphological differences support that optimal intragranular κ-carbide precipitation homogenizes plastic deformation and impedes fatigue crack propagation, whereas over-aging and intergranular precipitation degrade fatigue crack resistance.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of rapid fracture zone morphologies for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under fatigue loading, comparing solution-treated (ST) and aged samples (550-6, 600-6, 650-6) at stress amplitudes of Δσ/2 = 180 MPa (a–d) and Δσ/2 = 315 MPa (e–h). Subfigures (a) and (e) show the ST sample, (b) and (f) show 550-6, (c) and (g) show 600-6, and (d) and (h) show 650-6. The ST, 550-6, and 600-6 samples exhibit predominant dimpled fracture surfaces across both stress amplitudes, indicating ductile microvoid coalescence failure mechanisms. In contrast, the 650-6 sample displays mixed-mode fracture characteristics featuring both dimples and cleavage-faceted regions at both 180 MPa and 315 MPa, as visibly evident in (d) and (h). The caption and citation text report that this transition from ductile to mixed brittle-ductile fracture in 650-6 is attributed to extensive intergranular κ-carbide precipitation, which reduces ductility and promotes cleavage-like faceting, whereas the intragranular precipitation states in 550-6 and 600-6 preserve toughness. These results support the conclusion that the precipitation state of κ-carbides critically governs fatigue damage tolerance and final fracture behavior.

This figure presents EBSD characterization of fatigue crack propagation paths in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel tested at a stress amplitude of 225 MPa. Panels (a, d, g, j) are band contrast and inverse pole figure (IPF) superimposed maps for the solution-treated (ST) and aged 550-6, 600-6, and 650-6 samples, respectively, showing transgranular crack paths along slip bands with increasing tortuosity and microbranching in the aged conditions. Panels (b, e, h, k) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps revealing local plastic strain fields; the ST sample shows intense grain-boundary strain localization, the 600-6 sample exhibits a more diffuse distribution, and the 650-6 sample displays localized KAM hot spots at grain boundaries (marked by red arrows in k). Panels (c, f, i, l) are Schmid factor (SF) maps for the {111}<110> slip system (scale 0.0–0.5), indicating crack nucleation in hard-oriented grains with low SF and propagation into soft-oriented grains with high SF. The figure collectively supports that intragranular κ-carbide precipitation enhances crack-path tortuosity and crack-propagation resistance, whereas pronounced intergranular precipitation in the 650-6 sample reintroduces detrimental strain localization.

Figure 10 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of surface deformation morphologies on Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel after fatigue testing. The caption identifies subfigures (a) and (b) as the solution-treated (ST) sample, and (c) and (d) as the 600-6 sample (aged at 600 °C for 6 h), tested at stress amplitudes of Δσ/2 = 180 MPa (a,c) and 315 MPa (b,d). Visually, (a) shows wavy slip traces with arrows marking cross-slip steps, while (b) exhibits a broad curved deformation zone labeled as a kink band. In contrast, (c) and (d) display sets of straight, parallel traces labeled slip bands, with dashed purple lines indicating intersecting slip systems. The accompanying text states that the ST sample undergoes irreversible wavy slip and cross slip at 180 MPa, progressing to severe kink-band formation at 315 MPa that signals localized deformation and diminished deformability. Conversely, the 600-6 sample exhibits planar slip bands at both stress levels due to glide plane softening promoted by intragranular κ-carbides; at 315 MPa the band spacing decreases and multiple slip systems activate cooperatively, accommodating strain more uniformly via a largely reversible slip network. Collectively, the figure supports the core conclusion that intragranular κ-carbide precipitation suppresses catastrophic strain localization and enhances fatigue deformation response.

Bright-field TEM micrographs display post-fatigue deformation microstructures in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel for solution-treated (ST) and aged (550-6, 600-6, 650-6) samples tested at stress amplitudes Δσ/2 = 180 MPa (a, c, e, g) and 315 MPa (b, d, f, h). Subfigures (a) and (b) of the ST condition show persistent Lüder's bands (PLBs) marked by red arrows, reflecting severe cyclic strain localization. In contrast, aged specimens (c–h) exhibit planar slip bands indicated by blue dashed lines, demonstrating a transition to homogeneous planar slip mediated by intragranular κ-carbide precipitation. At the higher stress amplitude of 315 MPa, the planar slip bands appear denser and more narrowly spaced than at 180 MPa. The micrographs collectively illustrate that nanoscale κ-carbide precipitation suppresses the PLB-type strain localization observed in the ST state and promotes organized planar slip, which supports the improved fatigue resistance in optimally aged microstructures.

Figure 12 presents a schematic diagram comparing the slip reversibility mechanisms in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under solution-treated (ST) and aged conditions. Panel (a) illustrates the ST condition where short-range ordering (SRO) within the austenitic matrix causes glide plane softening; however, the relatively weak and spatially heterogeneous SRO structure is prematurely destroyed by repeated dislocation shearing, leading to localized absence of order, non-uniform softening across potential slip planes, strain concentration, and consequently low slip reversibility. Panel (b) depicts the aged condition where coherent ordered L1₂-structured intragranular κ-carbides are uniformly dispersed. When moving dislocations shear these precipitates, anti-phase boundaries (APBs) are created; subsequent dislocations traverse the same softened planes with reduced resistance because additional APBs are no longer formed. This path-dependent process channels dislocation activity to a limited set of previously softened glide planes, strengthens slip planarity, suppresses cross-slip, and significantly improves the reversibility of dislocation motion under cyclic loading, thereby reducing accumulated damage per cycle. This mechanism rationalizes the superior fatigue performance of the aged samples relative to the ST samples.

This figure presents TEM analysis of dislocation-κ-carbide interactions in fatigued Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel at a stress amplitude of 180 MPa. Panels (a) and (b) are bright-field TEM images comparing the 550-6 and 650-6 aged samples, respectively, revealing intragranular κ-carbides (marked by green circles) and dislocation lines (indicated by blue arrows). Panels (c) and (d) show HRTEM images of κ-carbides and dislocations in the 550-6 and 650-6 samples, with (c') and (d') providing magnified views of the green-boxed regions. Panels (c'') and (d'') display corresponding IFFT-filtered images where κ-carbides, austenitic matrix, and transition regions are highlighted in white, black, and blue, respectively. Red arrows in (c'') and (d'') indicate shear steps and localized contrast variations within the carbides, signifying disruption of the ordered structure and formation of anti-phase boundaries (APBs). The figure demonstrates that intragranular κ-carbides with average sizes of approximately 11 nm (550-6) and 24 nm (650-6) remain in the shearing regime, supporting the mechanism that repeated dislocation shearing causes mechanical dissolution of κ-carbides during cyclic loading.

Figure 14 presents bright-field TEM micrographs capturing deformation microstructures adjacent to grain boundaries in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel after fatigue at Δσ/2 = 315 MPa. Panel (a) shows the 550-6 sample (aged at 550 °C for 6 h), where uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbides facilitate well-organized planar slip bands, enabling relatively homogeneous slip transfer across the grain boundary. Panel (b) shows the 650-6 sample (aged at 650 °C for 6 h), revealing coarse intergranular κ-carbide precipitation disrupting grain boundary continuity; these brittle particles act as strong barriers to dislocation motion, intensifying dislocation pile-ups and promoting tangled dislocation structures with wavy-slip patterns. The direct comparison demonstrates that the κ-carbide precipitation state critically governs fatigue deformation mechanisms: fine intragranular precipitates promote coordinated planar slip, whereas coarse intergranular precipitation elevates local stress concentration and accelerates crack nucleation, shortening fatigue life.

This figure presents a schematic illustration depicting the interaction of planar slip bands with a grain boundary (GB) in aged Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel, contrasting fatigue deformation behavior across a soft-oriented grain (left, beige background) and a hard-oriented grain (right, light-blue background). A prominent red vertical line denotes the GB separating the two regions. In the soft-oriented grain, multiple parallel planar slip bands, drawn as dashed lines adorned with T-shaped dislocation symbols, traverse the grain interior and impinge upon the boundary. Upon crossing the GB, the slip bands refract and continue along altered trajectories within the hard-oriented grain, visualizing crystallographic slip transfer and orientation-dependent impedance. The diagram abstractly captures how dislocation motion and planar slip are blocked or redirected at grain boundaries, directly supporting the paper's mechanistic analysis of fatigue damage. It illustrates that heterogeneous grain orientation and boundary precipitation states critically govern strain localization, dislocation pile-up, and crack nucleation during cyclic deformation of κ-carbide-strengthened austenitic steel.

This schematic diagram illustrates the cyclic deformation mechanism across a grain boundary (GB) in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel, specifically highlighting the detrimental role of intergranular κ-carbides. The image depicts a soft-oriented grain (left, pink background) and a hard-oriented grain (right, blue background) separated by a GB decorated with coarse intergranular κ-carbides (yellow blocks). Dislocations gliding on slip planes (indicated by dashed lines with 'T' symbols) in the soft-oriented grain are blocked by these grain-boundary precipitates, resulting in pronounced dislocation pile-ups (magenta arrows) at the interface. The yellow arrows indicate that the intergranular κ-carbides act as strong barriers to slip transfer, intensifying local stress concentration. The caption states this figure compares absence (a) and presence (b) of intergranular κ-carbides. This visualization supports the core conclusion that pronounced intergranular κ-carbide precipitation—such as in the over-aged 650 °C condition—disrupts grain-boundary integrity, impedes homogeneous plastic deformation, and promotes early fatigue crack nucleation, thereby undermining the beneficial strengthening effect of intragranular κ-carbides.
